subject: Chemistry
title: Schrödinger's Electron Cloud
description: A tiny lesson with a big payoff: Schrödinger's Electron Cloud.

hook.text: Electrons don't orbit like planets. They exist as probability clouds—you can never know exactly where one is.

buildup.text: In 1926, Erwin Schrödinger wrote an equation that describes electrons as waves, not particles in fixed orbits.

discovery.text: The wave function gives the probability of finding an electron at any point. Dense cloud means high probability.

twist.text: Electron orbitals have bizarre shapes: spheres (s), dumbbells (p), clovers (d), and complex flowers (f).

climax.text: These orbital shapes determine how atoms bond. All of chemistry flows from the geometry of electron clouds.

punchline.text: Not orbits but clouds. Probability replaces certainty. Chemistry got fuzzy.

quiz.question: What does Schrödinger's equation tell us about electrons?
options: The probability of finding an electron at any given point in space
The exact position and speed of each electron
How fast electrons orbit the nucleus
